text: This video shows a robotic Fruchmann-Goldberg procedure for complete rectal prolapse. The patient is a forty-nine-year-old woman with a history of chronic constipation and a previous hemorrhoidectomy. She had complained of rectal prolapse associated with incomplete bowel evacuation, mucus discharge, pain, and bleeding. A seven centimeters complete rectal prolapse was diagnosed. At the preoperative workup, the patient was found to have a redundant sigmoid colon and normal sphincter function.

The patient is positioned in a steep Trendelenburg with right tilt, and the operative field is explored using the Da Vinci XI robotic platform. The aortic bifurcation, common iliac arteries, and inferior mesenteric artery are visible. The uterus is secured to the anterior abdominal wall with a monofilament suture for optimal exposure of the pelvic space.

The surgeon observes a deep pouch of Douglas and confirms the redundancy of the sigmoid colon. Using a tip-up fenestrated grasper, the recto-sigmoid mesentery is retracted from the pelvis. Dissection begins over the sacral promontory with a perineal incision on the right side of the mesentery. The mesorectum is mobilized posteriorly along the rectal proper fascia using sharp dissection with a monopolar hook, minimizing bleeding. Posterior mobilization continues down to the pelvic floor, with dissection of the right lateral attachments and division of the right lateral stalk. Stable tissue traction and counter traction, aided by the magnified three-dimensional view of the robotic platform, help preserve the pelvic autonomic nerves. Dissection is kept close to the rectal proper fascia to avoid injury to the hypogastric nerve.

The surgeon performs mesorectal dissection on the left side, making a peritoneal incision and dividing lateral attachments down to the previous dissection level. The rectum is completely mobilized, including division of lateral stalks, reducing prolapse recurrence risk. Dissection progresses cranially, retracting the mesozygnoid medially and dividing its V-shaped parietal attachment to create an opening in the mesentery. Lateral detachment continues along the white line of Toldt towards the descending colon, avoiding the splenic flexure. The colon is retracted by grasping the epiphylic appendices to minimize bowel injury risk.

The surgeon accesses the intersigmoid recess through an opening in the mesentery of the sigmoid colon. The superior rectal artery is identified as it courses toward the posterior aspect of the rectum. Mesenteric fat is cleared from the wall of the upper rectum at the planned transection site, preserving the blood supply from the superior rectal artery to reduce the risk of anastomotic leakage. The rectal wall is prepared using a bipolar electrosurgical dissector.

The surgeon transects the rectum using a 45mm robotic linear stapler with two blue reloads, ensuring a precise staple line. Following the transection, the mesazygmoid is divided while the sigmoid colon is suspended by grasping its proximal and distal ends. The division of the mesentery is performed using a bipolar dissector, proceeding from the rectal transection site up to the opening in the mesazygmoid, while preserving the superior rectal vessels.

The surgeon identifies the level for proximal bowel section by selecting a tension-free location for the anastomosis. A clip marks the section level, and the redundant sigmoid colon is removed. The colon is extracted through a suprapubic incision, and proximal transection is performed using a purse string device, completing the sigmoidectomy.

The anvil of a 29mm circular powered stapler is inserted into the lumen of the proximal colon. Endocyanin green fluorescence imaging is used to assess bowel stump perfusion. The stapler trocar extends and pierces the anterior rectal wall, coupling the anvil to the stapler. The mesentery orientation is checked, and extraneous tissue is excluded before firing the stapler to create an end-to-end colorectal anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a posterior suture rectopexy by stretching the right peritoneal flap toward the sacral promontory and suturing it to hold the rectum in an elevated position. Two interrupted 2-0 polyester stitches are placed through the peritoneal flap and prosacral fascia, using endo-wrist technology for suturing and tying. Care is taken to avoid injury to the prosacral veins, pelvic nerves, and retroperitoneal structures.

The surgeon approximates the incised peritoneal surfaces on the right side using a running suture with a 3-0 barbed slowly absorbable suture. The closure is performed in an oblique direction to enhance fixation of the rectum, allowing for posterior fibrosis.

The surgeon performs a suture on the left side, ensuring the rectum remains straight and firm. After completing the rectopoxy, several interrupted absorbable stitches are placed to prevent tension on the anastomotic suture line, which could occur due to straining in the early postoperative period.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the colorectal anastomosis using methylene blue dye and air insufflation to check for any leaks. A drain is then placed near the anastomosis site, and hemostasis is confirmed.
